questions.questionText: On MRI, T1-weighted images show fat as:
questions.optionB: Bright (hyperintense — fat has short T1 relaxation time, appears white on T1)
questions.explanation: Core concept: T1 = fat bright, water dark. T2 = water bright, fat intermediate. Why correct: Fat has short T1 relaxation → recovers longitudinal magnetization quickly → bright on T1. Water has long T1 → slow recovery → dark on T1. Why A wrong: Dark on T1 = water/fluid (CSF, cysts). Why C wrong: Muscle is intermediate on T1. Why D wrong: Only cortical bone/air have absent signal (no mobile protons). Clinical relevance: T1 = Anatomy (good contrast between tissues). T2 = Pathology (edema/inflammation = bright). Exam trap: Both fat AND gadolinium enhancement appear bright on T1 — use fat-suppression to differentiate. Memory aid: 'T1 = anatomy; FAT is PHAT (bright). T2 = pathology; WATER is WHITE (bright).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – MRI.
questions.conceptTag: T1 fat bright T2 water bright MRI

questions.questionText: Which MRI sequence is best for detecting periventricular white matter lesions in multiple sclerosis?
questions.optionB: FLAIR (Fluid Attenuated Inversion Recovery — suppresses CSF signal, making periventricular lesions conspicuous)
questions.explanation: Core concept: FLAIR = T2 with CSF suppression → periventricular lesions stand out. Why correct: On standard T2, CSF is bright and periventricular lesions may be obscured. FLAIR nulls CSF signal → lesions become bright against dark CSF. Ideal for MS plaques (Dawson's fingers). Why A wrong: T1 — plaques may be isointense or show 'black holes' (chronic). Why C wrong: DWI — for acute stroke, abscess. Active MS lesions may restrict but not the primary sequence. Why D wrong: Gradient echo (GRE/SWI) — for blood products, calcification, microbleeds. Clinical relevance: FLAIR also useful for subarachnoid hemorrhage, meningitis (leptomeningeal enhancement). Exam trap: FLAIR is NOT useful for posterior fossa lesions (poor CSF suppression near brainstem) — use T2 instead. Memory aid: 'FLAIR = Fluid suppressed, Lesions Around ventricles become Immediately Recognizable.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – MRI.

questions.questionText: MRI of the knee shows disruption of a low-signal band in the intercondylar notch that normally appears as a continuous dark structure on all sequences. Which structure is torn?
questions.optionB: Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL — normally dark on all sequences; disruption/high signal indicates tear)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Normal ACL = continuous dark band on MRI; disruption = tear. Why correct: ACL runs from lateral femoral condyle to anterior tibial plateau through intercondylar notch. Normal: low signal (dark) on all sequences (ligament fiber). Torn: discontinuity, abnormal signal, abnormal orientation. Why A wrong: Meniscus tear = intrameniscal signal reaching articular surface. Why C wrong: PCL is thicker, darker, more easily seen in the posterior intercondylar area. Why D wrong: MCL is medial, not in intercondylar notch. Clinical relevance: MRI is gold standard for ACL diagnosis (>95% accuracy). Bone bruise pattern: lateral compartment contusions (pivot-shift mechanism). Exam trap: ACL is best seen on sagittal oblique MRI along its course. Signal change WITHOUT discontinuity = partial tear. Memory aid: 'ACL = Anterior Cross-band, Lost dark signal = torn.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – MRI.
questions.conceptTag: ACL tear MRI dark band disruption knee

questions.questionText: On MRI of the spine, a disc herniation compressing the L4-L5 nerve root would be seen at which level?
questions.optionB: L4-L5 disc level (posterolateral herniation at L4-L5 compresses the traversing L5 nerve root)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Posterolateral disc herniation compresses the TRAVERSING (lower) nerve root. Why correct: At L4-L5, a posterolateral herniation compresses L5 root (traversing root exits at L5-S1 foramen below). The L4 root (exiting root) exits the L4-L5 foramen and is usually not affected by posterolateral herniation. Why A wrong: L3-L4 disc would compress L4 root. Why C wrong: L5-S1 disc would compress S1 root. Why D wrong: L2-L3 disc would compress L3 root. Clinical relevance: MRI is gold standard for disc herniation. L5 radiculopathy: foot drop (tibialis anterior weakness), big toe dorsiflexion weakness. Exam trap: FAR LATERAL disc herniation compresses the EXITING (upper) root — L4-L5 far lateral → L4 root. Memory aid: 'Posterolateral herniation → lower root compressed. Far lateral → upper root compressed.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – MRI.
questions.conceptTag: Disc herniation traversing root MRI spine

questions.questionText: Gadolinium-based contrast agents used in MRI are contraindicated in patients with:
questions.optionB: Severe renal insufficiency (eGFR <30 — risk of nephrogenic systemic fibrosis/NSF, a debilitating fibrosing condition)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Gadolinium + severe renal failure → risk of NSF. Why correct: NSF — fibrotic condition of skin, joints, internal organs, potentially fatal. Occurs with gadolinium exposure in patients with eGFR <30. Linear gadolinium agents (higher risk) vs macrocyclic (lower risk). Why A wrong: Iodine allergy relevant for CT contrast, NOT gadolinium (different mechanism). Why C wrong: Asthma — risk factor for CT contrast reactions, not gadolinium. Why D wrong: Pregnancy is a relative contraindication (use only if essential), not absolute like severe renal failure. Clinical relevance: Screen renal function before gadolinium. Macrocyclic agents (gadobutrol) preferred. Exam trap: Iodinated contrast (CT) → contrast-induced nephropathy. Gadolinium (MRI) → NSF. Different agents, different complications. Memory aid: 'Gadolinium + GFR<30 = NSF (Not So Fine for kidneys).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – MRI.
questions.conceptTag: Gadolinium NSF renal failure MRI contrast

questions.optionB: Non-MRI-conditional cardiac pacemaker/defibrillator (ferromagnetic components — risk of device malfunction, lead heating, tissue damage)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Ferromagnetic implants are absolute MRI contraindications due to projectile/heating/malfunction risk. Why correct: Non-MRI-conditional pacemakers/ICDs contain ferromagnetic components → mechanical force (missile effect), electrical interference, lead tip heating. Also: ferromagnetic intracranial aneurysm clips, metallic foreign bodies (orbital). Why A wrong: Most dental fillings are non-ferromagnetic (safe but may cause artifact). Why C wrong: Titanium is non-ferromagnetic (MRI safe — causes artifact but not dangerous). Why D wrong: Pregnancy — relative contraindication (avoid first trimester if possible, gadolinium avoided). Clinical relevance: MRI-conditional devices exist — can be scanned with specific protocols. Always screen patients before MRI. Exam trap: Stainless steel = ferromagnetic (UNSAFE). Titanium = non-ferromagnetic (safe). Know the difference. Memory aid: 'Pacemaker in MRI = P is for Prohibited.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – MRI.
